questions.question:
In a 2 x 4 rectangle grid shown below, each cell is a rectangle. How many rectangles can be observed in the grid?

[2 rows, 4 columns of cells]

A. 21  B. 27  C. 30  D. 36
questions.to_find: total number of distinct rectangles in a 2x4 grid

understand.plain: A 2x4 grid is formed by 3 horizontal lines and 5 vertical lines. Any rectangle is uniquely determined by choosing 2 of the 3 horizontal lines and 2 of the 5 vertical lines. We count all such pairs, including small cells, partial rows, and the full grid.

keywords.explain: a rectangle is formed by picking 2 horizontal lines and 2 vertical lines; the enclosed region is the rectangle
keywords.example: choosing lines y=0 and y=1 with x=0 and x=2 gives a 1x2 rectangle

keywords.explain: a 2x4 grid has 2 rows, so there are 2+1 = 3 horizontal lines
keywords.example: lines at the top, middle, and bottom of the 2-row grid

keywords.explain: a 2x4 grid has 4 columns, so there are 4+1 = 5 vertical lines
keywords.example: lines at positions 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 from left to right

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='120' viewBox='0 0 340 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='300' height='80' class='bg'/><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='320' y2='60' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='95' y1='20' x2='95' y2='100' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='20' x2='170' y2='100' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='245' y1='20' x2='245' y2='100' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='170' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2 rows x 4 columns: 3 horizontal lines, 5 vertical lines</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 2-row by 4-column grid showing 3 horizontal and 5 vertical lines

given.aim: count all rectangles (of any size) that can be formed in a 2x4 grid

terms.meaning: a grid with 2 rows and 4 columns of unit cells
terms.example: think of a 2-row, 4-column table
terms.connects: produces 3 horizontal and 5 vertical lines

terms.meaning: ways to choose 2 horizontal lines from 3
terms.connects: each pair of horizontal lines defines the top and bottom of a rectangle

terms.meaning: ways to choose 2 vertical lines from 5
terms.connects: each pair of vertical lines defines the left and right sides of a rectangle

given.plan: count pairs of horizontal lines and pairs of vertical lines independently, then multiply by product rule

steps.title: Count horizontal-line pairs
steps.apply: Rows = 2, so horizontal lines = 2+1 = 3. Choose 2: C(3,2) = 3!/(2!*1!) = 3.
steps.note: pairs are: (top,mid), (top,bot), (mid,bot)

steps.title: Count vertical-line pairs
steps.apply: Columns = 4, so vertical lines = 4+1 = 5. Choose 2: C(5,2) = 5!/(2!*3!) = 10.
steps.note: pairs span widths of 1, 2, 3, or 4 columns

steps.title: Apply product rule
steps.apply: Total rectangles = C(3,2) x C(5,2) = 3 x 10 = 30.
steps.note: every horizontal pair combined with every vertical pair gives a unique rectangle

questions.formula_note: choosing 2 lines from each direction via combinations, then multiplying, covers every rectangle of every size in the grid

questions.question:
Arun, Gulab, Neel and Shweta must choose one shirt each from a pile of four shirts coloured red, pink, blue and white respectively. Arun dislikes the colour red and Shweta dislikes the colour white. Gulab and Neel like all the colours. In how many different ways can they choose the shirts so that no one gets a shirt in a colour he or she dislikes?

A. 21  B. 18  C. 16  D. 14
questions.to_find: number of ways to assign one shirt each such that Arun avoids red and Shweta avoids white

understand.plain: There are 4 people and 4 distinct shirts (one each). Without restrictions there are 4! = 24 assignments. We subtract those where Arun gets red or Shweta gets white using inclusion-exclusion. Gulab and Neel have no restrictions.

keywords.explain: |A union B| = |A| + |B| - |A intersect B|; here A = 'Arun gets red', B = 'Shweta gets white'
we ban Arun from 1 shirt and Shweta from 1 different shirt; remaining 2 people are free
keywords.example: if Arun takes red, the other 3 (incl Shweta) freely arrange 3 shirts: 3! = 6 ways

understand.visual_alt: four-person, four-shirt assignment with two forbidden pairings

given.aim: count permutations of 4 shirts to 4 people avoiding 2 specific forbidden assignments

terms.meaning: all bijections from {Arun, Gulab, Neel, Shweta} to {red, pink, blue, white}
terms.connects: this is the starting count before applying restrictions

terms.meaning: the remaining 3 people freely choose from 3 shirts
terms.connects: these 6 arrangements must be excluded

terms.meaning: the remaining 3 people freely choose from 3 shirts
terms.connects: these 6 arrangements must also be excluded

terms.term: A intersect B: Arun gets red AND Shweta gets white
terms.meaning: the remaining 2 people freely arrange 2 shirts
terms.example: |A intersect B| = 2! = 2
terms.connects: these 2 cases are subtracted once extra by inclusion-exclusion, so they must be added back

steps.title: Unrestricted total
steps.apply: 4 people, 4 distinct shirts: total = 4! = 24.
steps.note: every bijection counts

steps.title: Subtract: Arun gets red (|A|)
steps.apply: Fix Arun with red; remaining 3 people arrange 3 shirts freely: 3! = 6.

steps.title: Subtract: Shweta gets white (|B|)
steps.apply: Fix Shweta with white; remaining 3 arrange 3 shirts freely: 3! = 6.

steps.title: Add back double-counted: both happen (|A intersect B|)
steps.apply: Fix Arun with red AND Shweta with white; remaining 2 (Gulab, Neel) arrange 2 shirts: 2! = 2.
steps.note: |A intersect B| = 2

steps.title: Apply inclusion-exclusion
steps.apply: Invalid = |A| + |B| - |A intersect B| = 6 + 6 - 2 = 10. Valid = 24 - 10 = 14.
steps.note: 14 valid assignments remain

questions.formula_note: inclusion-exclusion removes assignments where Arun gets red or Shweta gets white, with the overlap added back to avoid double subtraction

questions.question:
In how many ways can 2 distinct scholarships be awarded to 4 applicants, when each scholarship is given to a different applicant?

A. 4  B. 12  C. 64  D. 51
questions.to_find: number of ways to award 2 distinct scholarships to 4 applicants (one scholarship per applicant)

understand.plain: We have 2 distinct scholarships (scholarship S1 and scholarship S2) and 4 applicants. Each scholarship must go to a different applicant. The order matters because the scholarships are distinguishable. This is a permutation: we pick an ordered sequence of 2 applicants from 4.

keywords.explain: an ordered arrangement; P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)! counts ways to pick r items in order from n
keywords.example: P(4,2) = 4*3 = 12: the first scholarship has 4 choices, the second has 3 remaining

keywords.explain: because S1 and S2 are different prizes, giving S1 to Alice and S2 to Bob is NOT the same as S1 to Bob and S2 to Alice
keywords.example: order matters, so we use permutation not combination

understand.visual_alt: two scholarship slots each independently filled from a pool of 4 applicants

given.aim: count ordered selections of 2 different applicants from 4 for 2 distinct scholarship awards

terms.meaning: pool from which scholarship recipients are chosen
terms.example: call them A, B, C, D
terms.connects: each is eligible for exactly one scholarship

terms.meaning: two distinct prizes to be distributed
terms.example: scholarship S1 (first prize) and S2 (second prize)
terms.connects: since scholarships differ, the assignment is ordered

terms.meaning: each applicant can receive at most one scholarship
terms.example: once A gets S1, A cannot also get S2
terms.connects: each successive scholarship has one fewer candidate available

given.plan: apply the r-permutation formula P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)! with n=4, r=2

steps.title: Identify n and r
steps.apply: n = 4 applicants, r = 2 scholarships. No applicant receives both.
steps.note: scholarships are distinct so order of assignment matters

steps.title: Compute P(4,2)
steps.note: first scholarship: 4 choices; second scholarship: 3 remaining choices

questions.formula_note: the permutation formula P(n,r) directly counts ordered assignments of r distinct prizes to r of n candidates

questions.question:
Ten teams participate in a tournament. Every team plays each of the other teams twice. The total number of matches to be played is:

A. 20  B. 45  C. 60  D. 90
questions.to_find: total number of matches in a round-robin tournament where each pair plays twice

understand.plain: In a round-robin tournament with 10 teams, every pair of teams must play each other. If each pair plays once, the number of matches is C(10,2) = 45. Since each pair plays twice, the total doubles to 90.

keywords.explain: every participant plays every other participant; no team is excluded from any matchup
each pair of teams meets two times (home and away, or two legs), so every pairing contributes 2 matches
keywords.example: team A vs team B counts as 2 separate matches

understand.visual_alt: 10 teams in a round-robin with each pair meeting twice

given.aim: compute total matches when 10 teams each play all others twice

terms.meaning: the number of participating teams
terms.example: label teams T1 through T10
terms.connects: each team plays n-1 = 9 opponents

terms.meaning: number of unique pairs of teams
terms.example: C(10,2) = 10!/(2!*8!) = 45 distinct matchups
terms.connects: each pair plays once gives 45 matches

terms.meaning: each pair plays twice, doubling the match count
terms.example: 45 pairs x 2 = 90 total matches
terms.connects: multiplied by the product rule

given.plan: compute C(10,2) for unique pairs, then multiply by 2 since each pair plays twice

steps.title: Count unique team pairs
steps.note: each combination represents one matchup between two teams

steps.title: Multiply for double round-robin
steps.apply: Each pair plays twice: total matches = 45 x 2 = 90.
steps.note: the two games between the same pair are counted as separate matches

questions.formula_note: C(10,2) counts unique pairs; multiplying by 2 accounts for each pair meeting twice in a double round-robin

questions.question:
A five digit number is formed using the digits 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 without repeating any of them. What is the sum of all such possible five digit numbers?

A. 6666600  B. 6666600  C. 0000000  D. 0000000
questions.to_find: sum of all 5-digit numbers formed using digits {1,3,5,7,9} without repetition

understand.plain: Using the 5 distinct odd digits {1,3,5,7,9} without repetition, we can form 5! = 120 different five-digit numbers. Each digit appears in each position exactly 120/5 = 24 times. We find the contribution of each digit in each place value and sum them all up.

keywords.explain: by symmetry, each of the 5 digits occupies each of the 5 positions in exactly 4! = 24 numbers
keywords.example: digit 1 appears in the ten-thousands place in 4! = 24 of the 120 numbers

keywords.explain: a digit d in position p (ones=1, tens=10, ..., ten-thousands=10000) contributes d * 10^(p-1) per occurrence
keywords.example: digit 3 in the hundreds place contributes 3 * 100 = 300 per arrangement

understand.visual_alt: five-place value breakdown showing each digit's equal contribution to every position

given.aim: sum all 120 five-digit numbers formed from {1,3,5,7,9} with no repetition

terms.meaning: 5! = 120 permutations of 5 distinct digits
terms.connects: each permutation is one five-digit number

terms.meaning: each digit appears in each position 4! = 24 times
terms.example: digit 7 is in the units place in 24 of the 120 numbers
terms.connects: this symmetry lets us compute the sum by position

terms.example: all five digits together sum to 25
terms.connects: each position's total digit contribution is 24 * 25

given.plan: sum = (frequency per position) x (sum of all digits) x (sum of all place values) = 24 x 25 x 11111

steps.title: Count total permutations and frequency per position
steps.apply: Total numbers = 5! = 120. By symmetry, each digit appears in each position 120/5 = 4! = 24 times.
steps.note: every position is equally occupied by each digit

steps.title: Sum of the 5 digits
steps.apply: Sum of digits = 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9 = 25.
steps.note: all five digits are odd

steps.title: Sum of place values (repunit)
steps.note: the repunit 11111 captures contribution from all 5 positions

steps.title: Compute total sum
steps.formula_raw: total = n1 x n2 x n3
steps.note: each position contributes 24 * 25 as a digit value, scaled by its place value

questions.formula_note: by positional symmetry, each digit appears 4! = 24 times in each position, so the grand sum factors as 24 x (digit sum) x (repunit) = 24 x 25 x 11111

questions.question: How many four digit numbers can be formed with the 10 digits 0, 1, 2, ..., 9 if no number can start with 0 and repetitions are not allowed?
questions.to_find: count of 4-digit numbers using digits 0-9 without repetition where leading digit is not 0

understand.plain: We need four-digit numbers from digits {0,1,...,9} with no repeated digits and the leading digit cannot be 0. The leading digit has 9 choices (1-9); each subsequent digit is chosen from the remaining pool without replacement.

keywords.explain: a four-digit number cannot start with 0; the thousands digit must be 1-9
keywords.example: 0537 is a 3-digit number 537, not a 4-digit number

keywords.explain: once a digit is used in one position, it cannot appear in another position
keywords.example: if thousands digit is 5, then 5 cannot be used in hundreds, tens, or units

understand.visual_alt: four-digit number with positions showing 9 choices for first, then decreasing choices

given.aim: count 4-digit numbers from {0,...,9} without repetition and without leading 0

terms.meaning: the leftmost digit; must be 1 through 9 (9 choices)
terms.example: if thousands = 3, remaining digits are from {0,1,2,4,5,6,7,8,9}
terms.connects: eliminates 0 from the leading position

terms.meaning: second digit; chosen from 9 remaining digits (0 is now available)
terms.example: if thousands = 3, then hundreds can be any of the 9 remaining including 0
terms.connects: 9 remaining choices after fixing thousands digit

terms.meaning: tens has 8 choices, units has 7 choices after earlier selections
terms.example: with digits 3, 1 already used, tens can be any of 8 remaining
terms.connects: product rule combines all four independent choices

given.plan: multiply choices at each position: thousands = 9, hundreds = 9, tens = 8, units = 7

steps.title: Choose the thousands digit
steps.formula_raw: total = n1 x n2 x n3 x n4
steps.apply: Thousands digit: digits 1-9 are allowed (not 0), so 9 choices.
steps.note: this is the key restriction; only 9 of the 10 digits qualify

steps.title: Choose hundreds, tens, units digits
steps.apply: After fixing thousands digit, 9 digits remain (including 0). Hundreds: 9 choices, tens: 8 choices, units: 7 choices.
steps.note: the remaining 3 positions draw without replacement from 9 remaining digits

steps.title: Multiply all choices
steps.formula_raw: total = n1 x n2 x n3 x n4
steps.note: equivalently 9 x P(9,3) = 9 x 504 = 4536

questions.formula_note: the leading digit restriction reduces the first choice from 10 to 9, while the remaining three positions use ordinary no-repetition counting

questions.question:
Five teams have to compete in a league, with every team playing every other team exactly once, before going to the next round. How many matches will have to be held to complete the league round of matches?

A. 20  B. 10  C. 8  D. 5
questions.to_find: total number of matches in a single round-robin league of 5 teams

understand.plain: In a league where every team plays every other team exactly once, the number of matches equals the number of unique pairs of teams. With 5 teams, we choose 2 teams per match from 5, giving C(5,2) = 10 matches.

keywords.explain: each pair of teams meets exactly once; no home-and-away distinction
keywords.example: 5 teams: A vs B, A vs C, A vs D, A vs E, B vs C, B vs D, B vs E, C vs D, C vs E, D vs E

keywords.explain: the number of ways to pick 2 teams from 5 for a match; order doesn't matter since A vs B is the same match as B vs A

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='220' height='220' viewBox='0 0 220 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='193' cy='88' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='163' cy='185' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='57' cy='185' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='27' cy='88' r='18' class='hi'/><line x1='110' y1='30' x2='193' y2='88' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='110' y1='30' x2='163' y2='185' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='110' y1='30' x2='57' y2='185' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='110' y1='30' x2='27' y2='88' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='193' y1='88' x2='163' y2='185' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='193' y1='88' x2='57' y2='185' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='193' y1='88' x2='27' y2='88' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='163' y1='185' x2='57' y2='185' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='163' y1='185' x2='27' y2='88' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='57' y1='185' x2='27' y2='88' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='193' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='163' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='57' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='27' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: pentagon diagram with 5 team nodes and 10 edges representing all possible matches

given.aim: count unique pairings among 5 teams for a single round-robin

terms.meaning: total participants in the league
terms.example: teams labeled A, B, C, D, E
terms.connects: each must play every other exactly once

terms.meaning: combination choosing 2 teams per match from n total teams
terms.connects: division by 2 prevents double-counting (A vs B = B vs A)

terms.meaning: every team meets 4 opponents; 5 teams each play 4 matches
terms.example: total team-games = 5 * 4 = 20; but each match involves 2 teams, so matches = 20/2 = 10
terms.connects: alternative way to verify the answer using the handshaking analogy

given.plan: compute C(5,2) = n*(n-1)/2 for n=5 teams in a single round-robin

steps.title: Apply round-robin match formula
steps.apply: With n = 5 teams and each pair playing once: C(5,2) = 5*4/2 = 10.
steps.note: choosing 2 teams per match, order doesn't matter

steps.title: Verify via each team's match count
steps.apply: Each team plays 4 matches. Total team-appearances = 5 * 4 = 20. Each match has 2 teams, so matches = 20/2 = 10.
steps.note: both methods confirm 10 matches

questions.formula_note: C(5,2) counts all unordered pairs of teams; equivalently, 5 teams each play 4 opponents and each match is shared by 2 teams, giving 5*4/2 = 10
